threats.title: Genesis Depositors Can Steal Historical Gauge Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeV2._depositsForGenesis fails to update the user's reward checkpoint, allowing genesis depositors to retroactively claim rewards from past epochs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The userRewardPerTokenPaid mapping is crucial for calculating earned rewards. Leaving it at 0 for new deposits means the user's earned calculation will include the entire historical rewardPerTokenStored, draining the reward pool.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateReward(address(0)) is used instead of updateReward(_tokenOwner), leaving userRewardPerTokenPaid at 0

preconditions: Gauge has accumulated rewards over time (rewardPerTokenStored > 0)
Genesis pool is active and can call depositsForGenesis

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: userRewardPerTokenPaid is 0 for genesis depositors, maximizing the multiplier
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Depositor instantly earns (depositAmount * currentRewardPerToken) upon deposit

economic_questions: Can a user trigger a genesis deposit after rewards have been flowing?
Does the genesis pool allow arbitrary users to deposit at any time?
validation_requirements: Simulate a gauge with active emissions, then trigger a genesis deposit and immediately claim rewards.

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Amount needed to deposit into Genesis Pool
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Standard gas for deposit and claim
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to historical gauge emissions and deposit size

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires active gauge with distributed rewards
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: As late as possible before genesis pool closes, to maximize accumulated rewardPerToken
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Drains reward tokens intended for long-term stakers

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire accumulated reward token balance in the gauge

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check genesis pool deposit windows vs gauge emission start times

threats.title: Router Allows Draining Excess Pair Balances via Route Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: RouterV2.swapExactTokensForTokens uses a user-provided pair address for the initial token transfer, but uses the internally computed pair address for the actual swap execution, allowing attackers to bypass input transfers and drain excess pair balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can specify their own address as routes[0].pair, keeping their input tokens. The _swap function then executes on the real pair. If the real pair has excess balance (from direct transfers, rebasing, or failed swaps), it will swap that excess and send the output to the attacker.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeTransferFrom uses routes[0].pair while _swap uses pairFor(routes[i].from, routes[i].to)

preconditions: Target pair has excess token balance (balance > reserve)
Attacker can construct a custom route array

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker provides 0 input but pair uses existing excess balance to satisfy amountIn
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker receives output tokens without spending any input tokens

economic_questions: How much excess balance typically accumulates in production pairs?
Can an attacker artificially create excess balance to exploit this?
validation_requirements: Test swapExactTokensForTokens with a dummy contract as routes[0].pair and a real pair with excess balance.

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Equal to the excess balance in the target pair

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires pairs with excess balances (common with fee-on-transfer or rebasing tokens)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol or users who accidentally sent tokens to the pair
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All excess balances across all pairs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Analyze pairFor logic vs user-provided route struct
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify Pair.swap behavior when amountIn is derived solely from pre-existing excess

threats.title: Incorrect Math in getAmountInStable Breaks Routing and Pricing
threats.hypothesis_description: TradeHelper.getAmountInStable calculates the required input for a given output by adding the output to the reserve instead of subtracting it, returning completely incorrect values.
threats.investigation_rationale: This function is used to calculate reverse-routing amounts. Adding the output to the reserve calculates the input needed to ADD tokens to the pool, not remove them. It also scales using the wrong decimals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint y_1 = reserveOut + output; (should be subtraction)

preconditions: Integrator or frontend relies on TradeHelper for stable pool routing

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Returns massively inflated or deflated required input amounts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not directly exploitable for profit, but causes severe DoS or bad trades for integrators

economic_questions: Which external contracts or frontends rely on this function?
Can this be used to trick a router into accepting a highly unfavorable trade?
validation_requirements: Compare output of getAmountInStable with actual required input calculated manually.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breaks stable pool routing for the entire ecosystem relying on this helper

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users relying on frontends using TradeHelper

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify math against standard stable swap invariant

threats.title: Infinite Loop in getAmountsIn Causes Out-of-Gas Reverts
threats.hypothesis_description: TradeHelper.getAmountsIn uses a uint loop counter that decrements to 0, underflows to type(uint).max, and continues infinitely since it's always >= 0.
threats.investigation_rationale: Any call to this function will consume all gas and revert, rendering reverse-path amount calculations completely non-functional for the protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--) with uint counter

preconditions: Caller attempts to use getAmountsIn for routing calculation

economic_questions: Is this function critical for frontend routing?
validation_requirements: Call getAmountsIn with any valid route array and observe OOG revert.

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: None needed, standard solidity underflow bug

threats.title: swapExactTokensForTokensSimple Always Reverts Due to Uninitialized Route Fields
threats.hypothesis_description: RouterV2.swapExactTokensForTokensSimple initializes a route struct but fails to set the pair and receiver fields, causing the swap to always fail or send tokens to address(0).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a core user-facing function for simple swaps. Because routes[0].pair is address(0), getAmountsOut will return 0, failing the slippage check. If bypassed, tokens are sent to address(0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: routes[0].from, to, stable, concentrated are set, but pair and receiver are omitted

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but users lose gas on failed transactions

economic_questions: Are users actively directed to this function by the frontend?
validation_requirements: Execute swapExactTokensForTokensSimple and observe revert or loss of funds.

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace execution path with address(0) pair

threats.title: Precision Loss in Fee Accumulation Permanently Locks Dust Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: In Pair._update0/1, the fee index increment rounds down. If totalSupply is large relative to the fee amount, the ratio loses precision, permanently locking 'dust' fees in the PairFees contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Over thousands of swaps, this rounding error accumulates. The tokens are transferred to PairFees, but the global index doesn't account for them, making them unclaimable by LPs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 _ratio = amount * 1e18 / totalSupply; (rounds down before adding to index)

preconditions: Pair has high totalSupply (e.g., meme coins with high decimals/supply)
Frequent small swaps generate small fee amounts

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Dust fees accumulate in PairFees without index tracking
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Value is locked, not directly exploitable for profit, but represents a continuous leak of LP yield

economic_questions: What is the expected magnitude of dust accumulation over 1 year for a high-volume pair?
validation_requirements: Simulate 10,000 small swaps on a pair with 1e24 totalSupply and measure locked dust in PairFees.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Reduces actual APY for liquidity providers

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Calculate precision loss bounds based on typical token decimals
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare PairFees balance vs sum of claimable fees

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Cause PairFees Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: Pair._update0 calculates the fee index based on the pre-transfer amount, but transfers tokens to PairFees using a standard transfer. For FoT tokens, PairFees receives less than expected, causing insolvency when LPs claim.
threats.investigation_rationale: The global index inflates based on the theoretical fee, but the actual backing in PairFees is reduced by the token tax. Late claimers will revert due to insufficient balance in PairFees.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol allows creation of pairs with Fee-on-Transfer tokens
threats.config_evidence: No explicit block on FoT tokens in PairFactory.createPair

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeTransfer(token0, fees, amount) is used, but index0 is incremented based on the pre-tax 'amount'

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: index0 += amount * 1e18 / totalSupply, but PairFees balance += amount * (1 - tax)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: PairFees contract becomes undercollateralized relative to LP claims
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: First LPs to claim get full amount; late LPs get nothing (transaction reverts)

economic_questions: Are FoT tokens officially supported or blacklisted by the frontend?
validation_requirements: Deploy pair with FoT token, execute swaps, have multiple LPs claim fees sequentially until one reverts.

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit, but causes loss for other LPs

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breaks fee distribution for specific pairs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if router FoT functions mitigate this (they don't, issue is in Pair)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Calculate insolvency rate per swap based on tax %

threats.title: TWAP Oracle Averages Reserves Instead of Prices, Allowing Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: Pair.current() calculates TWAP by averaging historical reserves and deriving the price from the average, which is mathematically flawed and highly susceptible to short-term manipulation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Averaging reserves (x and y) over time and then calculating price (y/x) is not the same as averaging prices over time. A large, short-term skew in reserves heavily impacts the average reserve, allowing an attacker to manipulate the TWAP price reported to external protocols.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Calculates average _reserve0 and _reserve1, then passes them to _getAmountOut to derive price

preconditions: External protocol (e.g., lending market) relies on Pair.current() or Pair.quote() for pricing
Attacker has sufficient capital to heavily skew pool reserves

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: price = f(avg(reserve0), avg(reserve1)) instead of price = avg(f(reserve0, reserve1))
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker skews reserves heavily for a short period, drastically altering the average reserve values
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Manipulated TWAP allows under-collateralized borrowing or unfair liquidations in integrated protocols

economic_questions: Which external protocols integrate with this specific TWAP implementation?
What is the capital requirement to move the average reserve by 10% over a 30-minute window?
validation_requirements: Mathematically model the difference between true TWAP and reserve-averaged TWAP under extreme slippage scenarios.

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: High (requires moving reserves significantly, likely via flash loan held across blocks)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends entirely on integrated protocol TVL
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Requires specific external integration to be profitable

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Lower liquidity pairs are easier to manipulate
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When external protocol updates its internal oracle state

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Analyze periodSize (1800s) impact on manipulation cost

threats.title: Rounding Error in GaugeExtraRewarder Can Lock User Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: In GaugeExtraRewarder._pendingReward, integer truncation can cause the calculated reward entitlement to be slightly less than the user's recorded rewardDebt, causing an underflow revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because accRewardPerShare is updated using integer division, minor rounding differences between the time rewardDebt was set and when pending is calculated can cause (user.amount * accRewardPerShare / ACC_TOKEN_PRECISION) to be less than user.rewardDebt, reverting any interaction with the gauge.
preconditions: User interacts with gauge shortly after a previous interaction
Specific token amounts and reward rates trigger the rounding edge case

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Subtraction without checking if term1 >= term2
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Accidental DoS of user's ability to withdraw or claim
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit, purely a denial of service / locked funds issue

economic_questions: How frequently does this rounding condition naturally occur with standard 18-decimal tokens?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test the pending calculation with various realistic amounts and time deltas to trigger the underflow.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Degrades UX and traps user funds temporarily

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Identify exact mathematical conditions for underflow

threats.title: Unbounded Referral Fee Can DoS All Trading
threats.hypothesis_description: PairFactory.setReferralFee lacks an upper bound check. If set above 10000 (100%), the referral fee calculation in Pair._update0/1 will exceed the total fee amount, causing an underflow that reverts all swaps.
threats.investigation_rationale: While requiring admin action, the lack of bounds checking on a critical fee parameter creates a single point of failure that can permanently halt all protocol trading if the admin key is compromised or a mistake is made.

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit, pure griefing/DoS by compromised admin

economic_questions: Is the feeManager a multisig or a single EOA?
validation_requirements: Call setReferralFee(10001) and verify that all subsequent swaps revert.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete halt of protocol trading volume

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review all fee setters for missing bounds

threats.title: Emergency Mode Bypasses Genesis Pool Maturity Locks
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis pool depositors are subject to a maturity lock, but the emergencyWithdraw functions in GaugeV2 do not check this lock, allowing early withdrawal if emergency mode is activated.
threats.investigation_rationale: Emergency mode is intended to protect funds, but it inadvertently breaks the economic commitment of genesis depositors. If an admin is pressured or compromised, they can enable emergency mode to allow whales to dump early.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing require(block.timestamp >= maturityTime[msg.sender]) in emergency functions

preconditions: Genesis pool is active with locked depositors

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Genesis depositors withdraw before intended lockup expires
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early dump of tokens before market expects liquidity unlock

economic_questions: What is the typical duration of a genesis maturity lock?
validation_requirements: Activate emergency mode and verify a genesis depositor can withdraw before maturity.

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Avoidance of price drop upon official unlock
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable based on token price action

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Unfair advantage to genesis depositors over retail buyers

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Retail token buyers expecting locked liquidity
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Price impact from unexpected early dumping

threats.title: Tautological Check Allows Setting Internal Bribe to Zero Address, Breaking Fee Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeV2.setInternalBribe uses a tautological check `_int >= address(0)` which always passes. Setting the bribe to address(0) causes claimFees to revert, permanently breaking LP fee distribution for that gauge.
threats.investigation_rationale: A simple admin typo can set the internal bribe to the zero address. Because claimFees attempts to approve and call notifyRewardAmount on this address, it will revert, trapping all accumulated fees in the Pair until the admin fixes the address.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: claimFees reverts on zero address interaction

economic_questions: How quickly can the admin respond to fix this if it occurs?
validation_requirements: Set internal bribe to address(0) and verify claimFees reverts.

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check all address setters for similar tautologies
